segments.caption: The video opens with a black screen, then transitions to the red Netflix logo on a gray background. It quickly shifts to a man in a blue shirt and dark jacket smiling in a well-lit, cozy living room with a fireplace and bookshelves. The scene cuts to a news studio with a woman in a white blazer speaking in front of a 'MONEY with Melissa Francis' backdrop, followed by a man in a suit on a green screen labeled 'THE ECONOMY'. Historical newspaper clippings appear, highlighting stock market records from 1976 and 2013. The video then shows a vintage black car driving through a suburban neighborhood, followed by a modern news studio with three people discussing the Dow closing near 26,000. A man in a red tie is shown speaking on Fox Business, then President Donald Trump delivering a speech at a podium with Vice President Mike Pence and another man seated behind him. The scene transitions to a black-and-white shot of a man and woman in a 1950s-style kitchen, then to a graph showing the US GDP growth rate from 1960 to 2020. The final frame displays a graph of US average hourly wages from 1985 to 2015, with data points labeled $20.10 and $22.49, sourced from the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

The video then shows a split-screen of an older man in a suit, seemingly reacting to market news, followed by a close-up of a large bell being rung, with the text 'The stock market, explained' appearing. The final segment transitions to a bright, playful setting: a young girl in a yellow shirt runs a lemonade stand, with a sign reading 'LEMONADE' and a banner 'EXPANSION PLAN.' She interacts with another girl in a green shirt wearing a top hat, labeled 'BANK,' and later holds a mock stock certificate for 'Lemonade Stand Corporation.' The scene ends with the girl signing the certificate, symbolizing an IPO, with the Netflix logo in the corner.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a child in a green shirt handing a dollar bill to another child, setting the stage for a playful, educational financial narrative. The scene quickly shifts to a girl in a yellow shirt holding a hand-drawn 'Lemonade Stand Corporation' stock certificate, which certifies ownership of one share. She then hands the certificate to a boy in a blue shirt, who places it on a table next to a pitcher of lemonade and a stack of money, symbolizing an initial public offering (IPO). The girl in yellow, now acting as the business owner, counts her earnings while a hand-drawn 'EXPANSION PLAN' banner with illustrations of a house and trees hangs above the stand. She then distributes money to other children, who receive envelopes labeled 'DIVIDEND,' illustrating profit sharing. The boy in the blue shirt, now holding a stack of certificates, smiles confidently as text overlays appear, declaring 'Jill is the SMARTEST girl' and 'Lemonade Stand thing is gonna BE HUG.' He then contemplates the future, with the text 'I can sell these shares for even MORE later on,' emphasizing the concept of stock appreciation. The setting is a clean, brightly lit studio with a neutral background, emphasizing the educational and metaphorical nature of the lemonade stand as a business model. The entire sequence uses simple props, hand-drawn signs, and expressive gestures to teach financial concepts like IPOs, dividends, and stock growth in a child-friendly manner.

The video returns to Lynn Stout, now in a different setting with a chandelier and ornate railing, continuing her commentary. The final sequence features historical black-and-white footage: a man in a white shirt and cap working in a factory, a man in a suit inspecting a large machine, and two men in front of a construction site, all suggesting the industrial roots of modern capitalism. Throughout, the Netflix logo remains in the top right corner, anchoring the segment as part of a documentary or educational series.

segments.caption: The segment begins with an animated graphic showing a colorful sphere composed of various company logos (IBM, P&G, GE, Walmart, ExxonMobil) connected to a central black circle with a white hand icon, symbolizing investment or selection. The sphere is labeled '$1 MILLION' as the hand icon points to it, suggesting a financial bet or prize. This transitions to a newspaper headline from USA TODAY dated January 2, 2018, stating 'Warren Buffett wins $1M bet against hedge funds and gives it to girls' charity,' highlighting a real-world financial event. The scene then shifts to a black-and-white portrait of John Maynard Keynes, followed by historical footage of him at a conference table with delegates from the U.S.S.R., United Kingdom, and Yugoslavia, emphasizing his role in economic policy. The narrative then introduces a vintage 'BEAUTY CONTEST' from Picture Post, March 10, 1940, where readers are invited to vote for their favorite among nine illustrated women. The contest is visually animated, with yellow circles highlighting selected portraits, and then transitions to a corkboard displaying the same portraits with vote counts pinned beneath them, showing the results. The winning portrait, #2, is circled in yellow and zoomed in, illustrating the concept of herd behavior and market speculation as described by Keynes. The segment concludes with the beauty contest layout reappearing, now with multiple portraits highlighted in yellow and green, reinforcing the idea of collective decision-making and market trends.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman in a black blazer, speaking with a serious expression in what appears to be a retail or office setting, with shelves and products visible in the background. The scene quickly transitions to a black-and-white newspaper clipping from The New York Times dated June 16, 1972, headlined 'Stocks Decline Sharply; Dow Tumbles 14.94,' indicating a financial downturn. The narrative then shifts to a woman in a black blazer, identified as Lynn Stout, Corporate Law Professor, standing in a grand, wood-paneled hall with chandeliers and ornate railings, speaking directly to the camera. This is followed by archival footage of Milton Friedman on The Phil Donahue Show in 1979, wearing a light suit and tie, engaged in a discussion with the host, who has white hair and is dressed in a dark suit. The video then displays an animated stakeholder diagram—a blue circle with yellow spokes labeled 'shareholders,' 'bondholders,' 'suppliers,' 'employees,' and 'community'—on a green background, illustrating corporate stakeholders. This is followed by a black-and-white newspaper article titled 'A Friedman doctrine—The Social Responsibility Of Business Is to Increase Its Profits' by Milton Friedman, with a caption referencing General Motors. The scene returns to Lynn Stout in the same grand hall, continuing her commentary. The final shot is a black-and-white archival clip from Wall Street in 1987, showing a man in a vest and tie, looking intently at a computer monitor, with the text 'WALL STREET, 1987' displayed on screen, suggesting a moment of financial tension or crisis.
